serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Mulligatawny Shorba is approximately 300 g (one bowl), typically served as a starter or soup course.

nutrient_source.title: Mulligatawny Soup (chicken-based, lentil-thickened) — estimated from ICMR-NIN component data and USDA FoodData Central analogous entries
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Mulligatawny Shorba (non-veg) as a composite dish. Values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for red lentil dal, chicken broth, and spice components, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for Mulligatawny soup (NDB 06416 / SR Legacy) which reports approximately 50 kcal per 100 g for a condensed-style preparation. Restaurant-style Mulligatawny with meat is richer; values adjusted upward to reflect added chicken/mutton and oil/ghee. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of lentil-based soups and mixed meat-lentil dishes — analogous estimate based on red lentil soup GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mulligatawny Shorba (non-veg). GI estimated at 30 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for red lentil soup (GI ~21–29) and mixed lentil dishes (GI ~26–30). The addition of chicken/mutton and spices does not materially raise GI. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: (30 × 5.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3) / 100 = 4.68, rounded to 4.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g; ~960 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single 300 g restaurant serving of Mulligatawny Shorba provides approximately 960 mg sodium, representing about 42% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~30) driven by lentil and protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mulligatawny Shorba has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 30 and a glycemic load of approximately 4.7 per serving, making it a suitable soup choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when sodium is managed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate sodium and saturated fat from meat and ghee
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the saturated fat content is moderate (~0.8 g per 100 g) and lentils provide beneficial dietary fibre and potassium, the high sodium per serving warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; lean chicken preparation and reduced ghee are advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mulligatawny Shorba (Non-Vegetarian) is a richly spiced South Indian-origin soup that has been widely adopted across North Indian, Anglo-Indian, and local cuisines throughout India and Singapore. The name derives from the Tamil words 'milagu' (pepper) and 'thanni' (water), reflecting its origins as a peppery broth. In its non-vegetarian form, the soup is prepared with a base of red lentils (masoor dal) simmered with chicken or mutton, onion, tomato, garlic, ginger, black pepper, cumin, coriander, turmeric, and curry leaves. The lentils are partially or fully blended to create a thick, velvety texture, and the meat adds body and umami depth. Some restaurant versions incorporate a small amount of coconut milk or cream for richness, and a squeeze of lemon juice is added at the end for brightness. The soup is typically served hot as a starter or soup course. Nutritionally, it provides moderate protein (~4.2 g per 100 g) from both lentils and meat, a low glycemic index (~30) due to the lentil base, and meaningful iron and B-vitamin content. Sodium is elevated in restaurant preparations (~320 mg per 100 g), which is a consideration for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal-friendly. It is a popular menu item at North Indian restaurants in Singapore, including Bombay Curry and Kebabs and Aryana North Indian Restaurant.
